Kandovan village is most photogenic in late afternoon when warm light hits the cone-shaped dwellings against the mountain backdrop
The Blue Mosque's remaining original tilework details reward close-up macro photography — bring a lens that can capture the intricate patterns
cultural_notes: Tabriz played a central role in Iran's Constitutional Revolution (1905-1911) — the city is deeply proud of its democratic heritage
The Azerbaijani identity here is strong; language, music, and food all reflect this dual Persian-Turkic heritage
Muharram ceremonies in Tabriz are among the most intense in Iran, with massive street processions over ten nights — a powerful cultural experience for respectful visitors
Tabriz has historically been one of Iran's most progressive cities; the women's rights and pro-democracy movements both found early champions here
metadata.getting_there: Tabriz International Airport (TBZ) has daily domestic flights to Tehran, Mashhad, and other cities, plus international routes to Istanbul (13 weekly flights), Dubai, Baku, and Baghdad. Overnight trains to Tehran take about 12 hours, with comfortable first-class sleeper cabins. The international train from Van, Turkey resumed in 2025 (Monday and Thursday departures). Buses to Tehran take about 8 hours via highway. The new bridge over Lake Urmia connects Tabriz to Urmia in just 1.5 hours.

description:
Tehran is the kind of city that grabs you by the collar and doesn't let go. Sprawling beneath the snow-capped Alborz Mountains, Iran's capital is a metropolis of nearly 17 million people where ancient bazaars sit in the shadow of modernist towers, and the smell of saffron kebab drifts through traffic-choked boulevards. This isn't a polished postcard destination — it's raw, loud, and endlessly fascinating. If you want to understand modern Iran, Tehran is where you start.

The city's roots stretch back millennia to the ancient settlement of Ray, but Tehran as you see it today is largely a product of the last two centuries. The Qajar dynasty made it the capital in 1786, and successive rulers reshaped it — Reza Shah with his wide European-style avenues, the revolution with its murals and renamed streets. The result is a city of layers: Golestan Palace's tiled splendor sits minutes from brutalist apartment blocks, and the Grand Bazaar's labyrinthine corridors open onto six-lane highways. You'll feel centuries colliding at every intersection.

As a visitor, expect controlled chaos with a warm heart. Tehranis are famously hospitable and surprisingly cosmopolitan — you'll find third-wave coffee shops, contemporary art galleries, and underground music scenes alongside traditional teahouses. The northern neighborhoods climb into the mountains and feel almost alpine, while the south hums with the energy of the bazaar district. The pace is relentless during the day, but evenings bring a different rhythm as families spill into parks and the mountains fill with hikers escaping the city heat.

The best way to experience Tehran is to embrace its contradictions. Spend a morning lost in the Grand Bazaar, then take the metro north to the leafy streets of Tajrish. Ride the Tochal telecabin up into the Alborz for jaw-dropping city views, then come back down for dinner at a traditional restaurant in Darband. Visit the National Museum and Treasury of National Jewels for context, and leave time to just wander — Tehran rewards those who get off the beaten path.

travel_tips: Tehran's metro is clean, cheap, and the best way to avoid legendary traffic jams — download the Tehran Metro map before you arrive
The city sits at 1,200m elevation and slopes upward from south to north; expect noticeable temperature differences between districts
Air quality can be poor, especially in winter — check daily pollution indexes and consider a mask on bad days
Fridays are the weekend in Iran; many museums close but parks and mountain trails come alive with local families
Currency exchange: use official exchange offices (sarafi) rather than banks for the best rates; the Grand Bazaar area has many
Tipping is not expected but appreciated; round up taxi fares and leave 10-15% at sit-down restaurants
Snapp and Tap30 are Iran's ride-hailing apps and far easier than negotiating with street taxis
Women should carry a scarf at all times; dress code is enforced more loosely in northern Tehran but strictly at religious sites
metadata.best_time_to_visit: Late March through May (spring) and September through November (autumn) offer the most pleasant weather. Summers are hot and dry with temperatures above 35°C, while winters bring cold, occasional snow, and the worst air pollution. The Nowruz holiday (Iranian New Year, around March 20) is magical but the city empties as residents leave on vacation.
food_specialties: Dizi (abgoosht) — a slow-cooked lamb and chickpea stew served in stone crocks, a Tehran institution
Tahdig — the crispy golden rice crust that Iranians fight over at every table
Ash-e reshteh — a hearty herb and noodle soup, especially good from street vendors
Lavashak — sour fruit leather sold at every corner kiosk, the perfect walking snack
Bastani sonnati — traditional saffron and rosewater ice cream with frozen cream chunks
day_trips: Tochal and Darband — hike the mountain trails above northern Tehran, accessible by telecabin or on foot from the Darband trailhead
Karaj and the Chalus Road — drive north through the Alborz Mountains toward the Caspian Sea on one of the most scenic roads in the Middle East
Rey (Shahr-e Rey) — explore the ancient ruins, Cheshmeh Ali spring, and the Shah Abdol-Azim shrine at the southern edge of the metro line
photography_tips: The rooftop of Golestan Palace and the mirror halls inside offer extraordinary reflective shots — visit early to avoid crowds
Head to the Tabiat Bridge at sunset for dramatic skyline views with the Alborz as backdrop
The Grand Bazaar's shafts of light cutting through dusty corridors are best captured mid-morning
cultural_notes: Tarof (performative politeness) is deeply embedded in Tehran culture — a shopkeeper may refuse your money three times before accepting; insist politely
Tea is the social currency of Iran; never refuse an offer of chai, and expect to drink several glasses a day
Political murals are everywhere — they're part of the urban landscape and photographing them is generally fine, but avoid military installations
Tehranis take immense pride in their hospitality; don't be surprised if strangers invite you to their home for dinner
metadata.getting_there: Most international visitors arrive at Imam Khomeini International Airport (IKA), about 50km southwest of the city center. The drive into town takes 45-90 minutes depending on traffic. Domestic flights and some regional services use Mehrabad Airport (THR), which is closer to the center. Overnight trains connect Tehran to most major cities including Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad. Long-distance buses depart from several terminals, with Beyhaghi (Argentina) Terminal serving the most routes.

description:
Yazd looks like it was sculpted from the desert itself. This ancient city rises from the central Iranian plateau in a maze of adobe walls, wind towers, and narrow alleys that have barely changed in centuries. Named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2017, Yazd is one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities — and walking its old quarter at dawn, when the mud-brick skyline glows amber and the only sound is a distant call to prayer, you'll understand why it earned that distinction.

What makes Yazd truly singular is its ingenious relationship with one of earth's harshest environments. The signature badgirs (wind towers) that punctuate the skyline aren't decorative — they're ancient air conditioning, channeling breezes down into homes and cisterns. Underground qanats carry water from distant mountains through hand-dug tunnels. Yakhchals stored ice in the desert long before electricity. This is a city that solved the problem of desert living with pure architectural brilliance, and it's also Iran's spiritual heartland for Zoroastrianism, the ancient Persian religion. The fire at the Atash Behram temple has supposedly been burning since 470 AD.

Yazd moves at a pace that matches its setting — slow, deliberate, and deeply peaceful. It's small enough to explore entirely on foot, and getting pleasantly lost in the old quarter's winding lanes is half the point. You'll stumble onto hidden courtyards, traditional confectionery shops (Yazd is famous across Iran for its sweets), and rooftop terraces with 360-degree views of wind towers and desert mountains. The locals are welcoming in that unhurried desert way, and many of the old houses have been converted into atmospheric boutique hotels with courtyards and underground rooms.

Start your exploration at the towering Jame Mosque, then let the old quarter's alleys pull you in whatever direction they please. Visit the Zoroastrian Fire Temple and the haunting Towers of Silence on the city outskirts. Catch a zurkhaneh (traditional gymnasium) session in the evening for a truly unique cultural experience. And save a night for a desert excursion — the stars from the dunes outside Yazd are some of the clearest you'll ever see.

travel_tips: The old quarter is a labyrinth by design — download an offline map or you will genuinely get lost (though getting lost here is half the fun)
Summer temperatures regularly exceed 40°C; visit between October and April, and carry water at all times regardless of season
Yazd is known as the City of Bicycles; renting a bike is a great way to explore the flat terrain beyond the old quarter
The Towers of Silence are about 15 minutes outside the city center; hire a taxi and combine the visit with the Zoroastrian Museum nearby
Traditional hotels (boutique guesthouses in restored houses) are far more atmospheric than modern hotels — book one with a rooftop terrace
Yazd's confectioneries are legendary; try baklava, qottab (almond-filled pastries), and pashmak (cotton candy) from shops around Amir Chakhmagh Square
The zurkhaneh (traditional gymnasium) sessions are a UNESCO-recognized cultural practice; attend the evening session near Amir Chakhmagh for an unforgettable experience
metadata.best_time_to_visit: October through April avoids the extreme desert heat. Spring (March-May) is beautiful but can bring sandstorms. Autumn (October-November) offers the sweetest spot — warm days, cool evenings, and clear skies perfect for desert excursions. Winter nights can drop below freezing, but days are sunny and pleasant. Avoid June through August unless you're prepared for 40°C+ temperatures.
food_specialties: Qottab — crescent-shaped almond and cardamom pastries deep-fried and dusted with powdered sugar, Yazd's signature sweet
Baklava-ye Yazdi — a distinct local variation, lighter and more fragrant than Turkish-style baklava
Pashmak — wispy threads of cotton candy flavored with rosewater or pistachio, unique to Yazd
Shouli polo — a local herb rice dish with dill and broad beans, a Yazdi home-cooking staple
Ash-e shouli — a Yazdi variation of the classic Persian herb soup, thicker and more aromatic than versions elsewhere
day_trips: Kharanaq — a crumbling ghost town of mud-brick buildings about an hour northeast, hauntingly photogenic and largely deserted
Chak Chak — a cliff-side Zoroastrian pilgrimage site nestled into a mountain face, about an hour northwest, with extraordinary desert-mountain views
Meybod — an ancient town with a 2,000-year-old castle, traditional yakhchals (ice houses), and a caravanserai-turned-restaurant, about 50km northwest
photography_tips: The rooftops of old quarter hotels offer the best panoramas of the wind tower skyline — shoot at sunrise when the adobe walls glow gold
The Jame Mosque's twin minarets are most dramatically lit at night and photograph beautifully against a deep blue dusk sky
Inside the old quarter, the play of light and shadow through narrow alley openings creates striking compositions throughout the day
cultural_notes: Yazd is home to Iran's largest Zoroastrian community; the religion predates Islam by over a thousand years and its symbols are woven into the city's identity
The Towers of Silence were used for sky burial (exposing the dead to vultures) until the 1960s — approach the site with respect
Yazdi hospitality often includes an offer of tea and sweets; refusing completely is considered impolite, so accept at least a cup
The city's desert architecture — badgirs, qanats, yakhchals — represents one of the world's most sophisticated traditional responses to extreme climate
metadata.getting_there: Yazd's Shahid Sadooghi Airport (AZD) has daily flights to Tehran and Mashhad, plus international flights to Dubai. It's about 10km west of the old town. The city sits on the Tehran-Kerman rail line with several daily trains, including an overnight service from Tehran (about 6 hours). Buses connect Yazd to Isfahan (4 hours), Shiraz (5 hours), Kerman (4 hours), and Tehran (8 hours). The bus station is on the outskirts; a public bus or cheap taxi will get you to the old town center.
